content:
# Leviticus Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 58r–58v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_002_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_002_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** When anyone brings a grain offering to the LORD, it must be of fine flour. They shall pour oil over it and lay frankincense on top of it, **2** then bring it to Aaron's sons the priests. The priest shall take a handful of the fine flour and oil, together with all the frankincense, and burn this memorial portion on the altar — a fire-offering, a fragrant aroma to the LORD. **3** What remains of the grain offering belongs to Aaron and his sons; it is most holy, taken from the LORD's fire-offerings.

**4** When you bring a grain offering baked in an oven, it must consist of unleavened loaves of fine flour mixed with oil, or unleavened wafers brushed with oil. **5** If your grain offering is prepared on a griddle, it shall be of fine flour mixed with oil and without leaven. **6** Break it into pieces and pour oil over it — it is a grain offering. **7** If your grain offering is prepared in a pan, it shall be made of fine flour with oil. **8** You shall bring the grain offering made from these ingredients to the LORD, and when it is presented to the priest, he shall carry it to the altar. **9** The priest shall take from the grain offering its memorial portion and burn it on the altar — a fire-offering, a fragrant aroma to the LORD. **10** What remains of the grain offering belongs to Aaron and his sons; it is most holy, taken from the LORD's fire-offerings.

**11** No grain offering that you bring to the LORD may be made with leaven, for you must not burn any leaven or any honey as a fire-offering to the LORD. **12** You may bring them to the LORD as an offering of firstfruits, but they must not be offered on the altar as a pleasing aroma. **13** Season every grain offering you bring with salt. Never let the salt of the covenant of your God be missing from your grain offering — you shall offer salt with all your offerings.

**14** If you bring a grain offering of firstfruits to the LORD, offer it as heads of grain roasted over fire, crushed from fresh grain. **15** Put oil on it and lay frankincense on it — it is a grain offering. **16** The priest shall burn as its memorial portion some of the crushed grain and some of the oil, along with all the frankincense — a fire-offering to the LORD.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Minchah — tribute more than recipe.** The chapter opens with the word *minchah*, which the same tradition uses for Jacob's lavish "gift" to Esau (Gen 32:14) and Joseph's brothers' "tribute" to an unknown Egyptian lord (Gen 43:11). When Israel brings a *minchah* to the LORD, it is not primarily a meal; it is an act of homage from a vassal to a sovereign. The grain offering is the poor person's burnt offering — it costs something real, but it places the bringer in the same posture before God as any other worshiper. PNEUMA renders it "grain offering" throughout to anchor the agricultural reality while the word's relational weight lives in context.

**Azkarah — memorial portion, not "reminder."** Verse 2 introduces *azkarah*, the small portion burned on the altar. The LXX renders this as *μνημόσυνον* (mnemosynon — memorial, remembrance-token). It is the part that "stands for" the whole: the handful of flour, oil, and all the frankincense rise in smoke as a representative act. PNEUMA translates "memorial portion" rather than the flat "token portion" of some modern versions, because *azkarah* is relational memory — it keeps the offerer present before God.

**No leaven, no honey — the prohibition on fermentation.** Verse 11 is striking in its dual prohibition: *se'or* (sourdough leaven) and *devash* (honey, or perhaps date syrup) are both banned from the altar because both ferment — they are agents of decomposition and corruption. The offering to God is to be uncorrupted, unaltered by any process that transforms the gift away from what it was when given. The LXX adds no further nuance here; the prohibition is plain in both traditions.

**Melach berit Elohekha — the covenant salt.** Verse 13 is theologically the most charged line in the chapter. Salt in the ancient Near East was the sign of a sealed covenant — permanent, preserving, incorruptible (the opposite of leaven and honey). "The salt of the covenant of your God" binds every offering into the larger covenant relationship. To omit the salt would be to offer outside the covenant — technically impossible, since there would be no "offering" in the covenantal sense at all. PNEUMA preserves the full phrase rather than softening it to "salt your offerings," because the covenant-weight is the entire point. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders it *ἁλὸς διαθήκης* (halos diathekis — salt of the covenant), confirming the Masoretic reading precisely.

**Firstfruits grain offering (vv. 14–16).** The *bikkurim* (firstfruits) offering in verses 14–16 is made from fresh-roasted grain, not finished bread — the grain at its most raw and immediate, straight from the field. This variety of grain offering captures harvest-moment gratitude before the crop is even fully processed. The LXX describes the grain as *χίδρα* (chidra), a Greek term for parched grain, confirming the Masoretic picture of grain roasted on fire. PNEUMA renders "heads of grain roasted over fire, crushed from fresh grain" to capture the immediacy and roughness of this most basic form of worship: you bring what you just harvested, still fragrant from the field, still hot from the fire.

**LXX (Vaticanus) observations.** Codex Vaticanus page 86–87 preserves the LXX text of Leviticus 2 in clean uncial script with minimal corrections. The Greek tracks the Hebrew closely throughout, with *θυσία* (thusia — sacrifice, offering) used as the umbrella term for *minchah*, a choice that slightly narrows the tribute/gift connotation of the Hebrew toward the sacrificial register. The Vaticanus reading at v. 13 (*ἁλὸς διαθήκης κυρίου τοῦ θεοῦ σου* — "salt of the covenant of the LORD your God") expands slightly on the Hebrew, adding *kyriou* (of the LORD) before "your God," making the covenant reference even more explicit. This expansion, while not in the MT, reflects an interpretive instinct consonant with the verse's theological purpose — and PNEUMA honors it by preserving the full covenantal phrase.

*— PNEUMA*
